title: Find maximum element in array
description: Given an array of integers return the maximum element

examples.explanation:
Array: [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]
Step 1: Check element 3 at index 0
  Compare 3 with 1: 3 > 1 ✓
  Compare 3 with 4: 3 < 4 ✗ → 3 is not maximum
Step 2: Check element 1 at index 1
  Compare 1 with 3: 1 < 3 ✗ → 1 is not maximum
Step 3: Check element 4 at index 2
  Compare 4 with 3: 4 > 3 ✓
  Compare 4 with 1: 4 > 1 ✓
  Compare 4 with 1: 4 > 1 ✓
  Compare 4 with 5: 4 < 5 ✗ → 4 is not maximum
Step 4: Check element 1 at index 3
  Compare 1 with 3: 1 < 3 ✗ → 1 is not maximum
Step 5: Check element 5 at index 4
  Compare 5 with 3: 5 > 3 ✓
  Compare 5 with 1: 5 > 1 ✓
  Compare 5 with 4: 5 > 4 ✓
  Compare 5 with 1: 5 > 1 ✓
  Compare 5 with 9: 5 < 9 ✗ → 5 is not maximum
Step 6: Check element 9 at index 5
  Compare 9 with 3: 9 > 3 ✓
  Compare 9 with 1: 9 > 1 ✓
  Compare 9 with 4: 9 > 4 ✓
  Compare 9 with 1: 9 > 1 ✓
  Compare 9 with 5: 9 > 5 ✓
  All comparisons passed → 9 is maximum
Result: 9

Array: [-5, -2, -8, -1, -3]
Step 1: Check element -5 at index 0
  Compare -5 with -2: -5 < -2 ✗ → -5 is not maximum
Step 2: Check element -2 at index 1
  Compare -2 with -5: -2 > -5 ✓
  Compare -2 with -8: -2 > -8 ✓
  Compare -2 with -1: -2 < -1 ✗ → -2 is not maximum
Step 3: Check element -8 at index 2
  Compare -8 with -5: -8 < -5 ✗ → -8 is not maximum
Step 4: Check element -1 at index 3
  Compare -1 with -5: -1 > -5 ✓
  Compare -1 with -2: -1 > -2 ✓
  Compare -1 with -8: -1 > -8 ✓
  Compare -1 with -3: -1 > -3 ✓
  All comparisons passed → -1 is maximum
Result: -1

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare every element with every other element to find the maximum. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Element Finder
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution comparing every element with every other element to find maximum.

explanation.prose: The brute force approach to finding the maximum element in an array works by exhaustively comparing each element against all other elements to determine if it's the largest. The intuition is simple: if an element is greater than or equal to every other element in the array, it must be the maximum. This approach doesn't require any sophisticated data structures or algorithms—just nested loops that perform pairwise comparisons. The algorithm starts by assuming the first element is the maximum, then for each element, it compares it against all other elements. If any element is found to be greater than the current candidate, that element becomes the new candidate. The process continues until all elements have been checked against all other elements. The .

connectsTo.reason: by implementing the complete nested comparison logic

timeComplexity.note: because for each of n elements, we compare against n-1 other elements, resulting in approximately n² comparisons

spaceComplexity.note: as we only use a few variables regardless of input size

commonMistakes: Not handling empty arrays (should return appropriate error or value)
Off-by-one errors in loop bounds
Incorrect comparison logic (using > instead of >= for duplicate maximum values)
Not resetting comparison flags properly
explanation.codePattern: nested loops where the outer loop selects each element as a candidate, and the inner loop verifies it against all others
algorithm: 1. If array is empty, return appropriate value (None, -inf, or raise error)
2. For i from 0 to n-1:
a. Set is_max = True
b. For j from 0 to n-1:
i. If i != j and array[j] > array[i]:
- Set is_max = False
- Break inner loop
c. If is_max is True:
- Return array[i]
3. Return last element (should never reach here if array has elements)

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]
Step 1: Check element 3 at index 0
  Compare 3 with 1: 3 > 1 ✓
  Compare 3 with 4: 3 < 4 ✗ → 3 is not maximum
Step 2: Check element 1 at index 1
  Compare 1 with 3: 1 < 3 ✗ → 1 is not maximum
Step 3: Check element 4 at index 2
  Compare 4 with 3: 4 > 3 ✓
  Compare 4 with 1: 4 > 1 ✓
  Compare 4 with 1: 4 > 1 ✓
  Compare 4 with 5: 4 < 5 ✗ → 4 is not maximum
Step 4: Check element 1 at index 3
  Compare 1 with 3: 1 < 3 ✗ → 1 is not maximum
Step 5: Check element 5 at index 4
  Compare 5 with 3: 5 > 3 ✓
  Compare 5 with 1: 5 > 1 ✓
  Compare 5 with 4: 5 > 4 ✓
  Compare 5 with 1: 5 > 1 ✓
  Compare 5 with 9: 5 < 9 ✗ → 5 is not maximum
Step 6: Check element 9 at index 5
  Compare 9 with 3: 9 > 3 ✓
  Compare 9 with 1: 9 > 1 ✓
  Compare 9 with 4: 9 > 4 ✓
  Compare 9 with 1: 9 > 1 ✓
  Compare 9 with 5: 9 > 5 ✓
  All comparisons passed → 9 is maximum
Result: 9

Array: [-5, -2, -8, -1, -3]
Step 1: Check element -5 at index 0
  Compare -5 with -2: -5 < -2 ✗ → -5 is not maximum
Step 2: Check element -2 at index 1
  Compare -2 with -5: -2 > -5 ✓
  Compare -2 with -8: -2 > -8 ✓
  Compare -2 with -1: -2 < -1 ✗ → -2 is not maximum
Step 3: Check element -8 at index 2
  Compare -8 with -5: -8 < -5 ✗ → -8 is not maximum
Step 4: Check element -1 at index 3
  Compare -1 with -5: -1 > -5 ✓
  Compare -1 with -2: -1 > -2 ✓
  Compare -1 with -8: -1 > -8 ✓
  Compare -1 with -3: -1 > -3 ✓
  All comparisons passed → -1 is maximum
Result: -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Element Dominance Checker
ladders.desc: Check if a given element is greater than or equal to all other elements in array.

explanation.prose: The element dominance checker is the core sub-routine of the brute force maximum finding approach. Given a specific element and its position in the array, this function determines whether that element is greater than or equal to every other element. The intuition is straightforward: an element is the maximum if and only if no other element in the array is strictly greater than it. This check requires comparing the candidate element against all other elements exactly once. The algorithm iterates through the array, skipping the candidate's own position, and checks if any element is greater than the candidate. If such an element is found, the candidate fails the dominance check. If the loop completes without finding any greater element, the candidate passes. This sub-routine connects to L0 by being the inner verification logic in the complete solution.

timeComplexity.note: for checking one element against n-1 others

commonMistakes: Forgetting to skip the candidate's own index (would always return true for >= comparison)
Using wrong comparison operator (should be > not >= for finding strict maximum)
Not handling empty arrays or single-element arrays correctly
explanation.codePattern: a simple loop that compares the candidate against all other elements
algorithm: 1. Input: array, candidate_index
2. candidate_value = array[candidate_index]
3. For i from 0 to n-1:
a. If i != candidate_index and array[i] > candidate_value:
- Return False (candidate is not maximum)
4. Return True (candidate is maximum)

time.note: Linear time for one element check

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 7, 2, 7, 1], Checking if element at index 1 (value 7) is maximum
Step 1: Compare with index 0 (value 3): 7 > 3 ✓
Step 2: Compare with index 2 (value 2): 7 > 2 ✓
Step 3: Compare with index 3 (value 7): 7 > 7? No (7 == 7) ✓
Step 4: Compare with index 4 (value 1): 7 > 1 ✓
No element found that is > 7
Result: True (7 is maximum, though not unique)

Array: [5, 2, 8, 3], Checking if element at index 0 (value 5) is maximum
Step 1: Compare with index 1 (value 2): 5 > 2 ✓
Step 2: Compare with index 2 (value 8): 5 > 8? No (5 < 8) ✗
Found element 8 > 5
Result: False (5 is not maximum)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two elements to determine their relative ordering.

explanation.prose: Pairwise element comparison is the fundamental operation at the heart of the brute force maximum finding approach. The core insight is that determining the maximum element in a collection ultimately reduces to comparing elements two at a time. By comparing each pair of elements, we can establish a partial ordering that eventually reveals the maximum element. The technique involves systematically comparing each element with every other element, creating what's essentially a tournament where each comparison determines which of two elements is larger. In the brute force O(n²) approach, this is done exhaustively: for n elements, we perform approximately n² comparisons. The intuition is that if element A is greater than element B, and element B is greater than element C, then A is greater than C (transitive property). However, the brute force approach doesn't leverage transitivity—it checks every pair independently. This core logic connects to L1 by being the atomic operation within the dominance checker. Time complexity for all pairwise comparisons is O(n²), but each individual comparison is O(1).
commonMistakes: Comparing elements with themselves (wasteful but not wrong)
Not handling equal elements correctly
Using inconsistent comparison logic
explanation.codePattern: a simple greater-than (>) comparison between two values

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 1, 4]
All pairwise comparisons (excluding self-comparisons):
Index pairs and results:
(0,1): Compare 3 and 1 → 3 > 1 ✓
(0,2): Compare 3 and 4 → 3 < 4 ✗
(1,0): Compare 1 and 3 → 1 < 3 ✗
(1,2): Compare 1 and 4 → 1 < 4 ✗
(2,0): Compare 4 and 3 → 4 > 3 ✓
(2,1): Compare 4 and 1 → 4 > 1 ✓
From these comparisons, we can see:
- 4 is greater than both 3 and 1
- 3 is greater than 1 but less than 4
- 1 is less than both 3 and 4
Thus 4 is the maximum element.

Array: [5, 5, 2]
All pairwise comparisons (excluding self-comparisons):
Index pairs and results:
(0,1): Compare 5 and 5 → 5 == 5 (equal)
(0,2): Compare 5 and 2 → 5 > 2 ✓
(1,0): Compare 5 and 5 → 5 == 5 (equal)
(1,2): Compare 5 and 2 → 5 > 2 ✓
(2,0): Compare 2 and 5 → 2 < 5 ✗
(2,1): Compare 2 and 5 → 2 < 5 ✗
From these comparisons:
- Both 5s are equal to each other
- Both 5s are greater than 2
- 2 is less than both 5s
Thus either 5 is maximum (there are two maximum values).

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Visit each element and compare it with all other elements using nested iteration.

explanation.prose: Array traversal with nested loops is the structural foundation that enables the O(n²) brute force comparison approach. This building block involves systematically accessing each element in the array and, for each element, accessing all other elements for comparison. The outer loop selects one element at a time as a candidate, while the inner loop iterates through all elements to compare against the candidate. The intuition is that to check if an element is the maximum, we need to examine its relationship with every other element, which naturally requires two levels of iteration. This pattern creates a complete pairwise comparison matrix (excluding the diagonal where an element compares with itself). The algorithm uses index variables to track positions in both loops, ensuring each pair (i,j) where i≠j is examined exactly once.

connectsTo.reason: by providing the iteration framework for performing pairwise comparisons

commonMistakes: Using the same index variable for both loops
Incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors)
Not skipping self-comparison (i==j)
Inefficient traversal order
explanation.codePattern: standard nested for-loops with index variables
algorithm: 1. n = length of array
2. For i from 0 to n-1:
a. For j from 0 to n-1:
i. If i != j:
- Access array[i] and array[j]
- Perform comparison operation

examples.trace:
Array: [2, 4, 6], n=3
Nested loop traversal (showing i,j pairs where i≠j):
Outer loop i=0:
  Inner loop j=0: i=j=0 → skip (self-comparison)
  Inner loop j=1: i=0,j=1 → compare array[0]=2 with array[1]=4
  Inner loop j=2: i=0,j=2 → compare array[0]=2 with array[2]=6
Outer loop i=1:
  Inner loop j=0: i=1,j=0 → compare array[1]=4 with array[0]=2
  Inner loop j=1: i=j=1 → skip (self-comparison)
  Inner loop j=2: i=1,j=2 → compare array[1]=4 with array[2]=6
Outer loop i=2:
  Inner loop j=0: i=2,j=0 → compare array[2]=6 with array[0]=2
  Inner loop j=1: i=2,j=1 → compare array[2]=6 with array[1]=4
  Inner loop j=2: i=j=2 → skip (self-comparison)
Total comparisons: 6 (n×(n-1) = 3×2 = 6)
Pairs examined: (0,1), (0,2), (1,0), (1,2), (2,0), (2,1)

Array: [1, 2], n=2
Nested loop traversal (showing i,j pairs where i≠j):
Outer loop i=0:
  Inner loop j=0: i=j=0 → skip
  Inner loop j=1: i=0,j=1 → compare array[0]=1 with array[1]=2
Outer loop i=1:
  Inner loop j=0: i=1,j=0 → compare array[1]=2 with array[0]=1
  Inner loop j=1: i=j=1 → skip
Total comparisons: 2 (n×(n-1) = 2×1 = 2)
Pairs examined: (0,1), (1,0)

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d, e], Count comparisons
Array: [p, q, r], Skip self, count

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its positional index.

explanation.prose: Array element access by index is the fundamental operation that enables all array algorithms, including the brute force maximum finder. This operation involves using an integer index to retrieve the value stored at a specific position in the array. Arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where elements are stored sequentially, and each element has a unique index starting from 0. The intuition is simple: if you know the position (index) of an element, you can directly access its value in constant time. This is possible because arrays provide random access—the time to access any element is the same regardless of its position. In the context of finding the maximum element, we need to access elements repeatedly for comparison: first to get candidate values, then to get other elements to compare against. The algorithm for accessing an element is straightforward: given an array A and index i, return A[i].

connectsTo.reason: by being the operation performed at each step of the nested loop traversal

commonMistakes: Using indices outside the valid range (0 to n-
Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing
Not checking array bounds before access
algorithm: 1. Verify index is within bounds: 0 <= index < length(array)
2. If valid: return array[index]
3. If invalid: handle error (return error value, throw exception, etc.)

examples.trace:
Array: [10, 20, 30, 40]
Indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40
Step 1: Check if index 2 is valid
  Array length = 4
  Valid indices: 0, 1, 2, 3
  2 is within 0 to 3 ✓
Step 2: Access element at index 2
  array[2] = 30
Result: 30

Array: [5, 15, 25]
Indices: 0:5, 1:15, 2:25
Step 1: Check if index 0 is valid
  Array length = 3
  Valid indices: 0, 1, 2
  0 is within 0 to 2 ✓
Step 2: Access element at index 0
  array[0] = 5
Result: 5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Ordered Collections
ladders.desc: Arrays as indexed sequences of elements where each element has a position.

explanation.prose: Understanding arrays as ordered collections is the foundational concept required for any array algorithm, including finding the maximum element. An array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type. The key characteristics are: 1) Elements are stored in contiguous memory locations, 2) Each element has an index (position) starting from 0, 3) The size is fixed when created (in many languages), 4) All elements are of the same data type. The intuition is that an array is like a row of lockers—each locker has a number (index) and contains one item (element). You can quickly go to any locker if you know its number. This concept is fundamental because the brute force maximum finding algorithm relies entirely on being able to access elements by their positions and compare them. Without understanding that arrays have ordered positions, the idea of comparing 'each element with every other element' wouldn't make sense. ' Common beginner mistakes include: 1) Thinking arrays start at index 1 (they usually start at 0), 2) Not understanding that array size is fixed, 3) Confusing arrays with other collections like lists. The real pattern is declaring an array with elements and understanding their positions.

connectsTo.reason: by establishing what it means to 'access an element by index

algorithm: 1. Declaration: array = [value1, value2, ..., valueN]
2. Indexing: First element at index 0, last at index N-1
3. Length: Number of elements in array
4. Element: Individual value at a specific index

examples.output: Array with 4 elements: index0=5, index1=10, index2=15, index3=20
examples.trace:
Array: [5, 10, 15, 20]
Step 1: Count elements
  Elements: 5, 10, 15, 20 → 4 elements
Step 2: Assign indices
  Index 0: first element = 5
  Index 1: second element = 10
  Index 2: third element = 15
  Index 3: fourth element = 20
Step 3: Verify
  Length = 4
  Valid indices: 0, 1, 2, 3
  Last index = length - 1 = 3
Result: Array of length 4 with indices 0-3

Array: [100]
Step 1: Count elements
  Only one element: 100
Step 2: Assign index
  Index 0: first (and only) element = 100
Step 3: Verify
  Length = 1
  Valid indices: only 0
  Last index = length - 1 = 0
Result: Array of length 1 with single element at index 0

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d], How many elements?
Array: [x, y, z], What is at index 0?

approaches.name: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array once while keeping track of the maximum element seen so far. This is the standard optimal solution for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Maximum Element in Array Using Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding the maximum element in an array by scanning through all elements once.

explanation.prose: The linear scan approach solves the maximum element problem by examining each element exactly once while maintaining a running maximum. Intuitively, this works because the maximum element must be at least as large as every other element, so by comparing each element to the current maximum candidate, we can guarantee finding the true maximum. The algorithm starts by initializing a variable (max_val) with the first element of the array (or a very small value if the array might be empty, though we assume non-empty arrays here). Then, it iterates through the remaining elements. For each element, it compares it to max_val. If the current element is greater, max_val is updated to this new value. After processing all elements, max_val holds the maximum. This is optimal .

connectsTo.reason: This full solution directly uses the key subroutine of comparing and updating the maximum

timeComplexity.note: because we must examine each element at least once to ensure we haven't missed a larger value, and O(1) space because we only store one extra variable

commonMistakes: not handling empty arrays (which we assume won't occur here)
initializing max_val incorrectly (e.g
with 0 when negative numbers exist)
or off-by-one errors in the loop range
explanation.codePattern: def find_max(arr): max_val = arr[0]; for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] > max_val: max_val = arr[i]; return max_val

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize max_val = arr[0] = 3. Step 2: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=7 > 3, update max_val=7. Step 3: i=2: arr[2]=2 <= 7, no update. Step 4: i=3: arr[3]=9 > 7, update max_val=9. Step 5: i=4: arr[4]=1 <= 9, no update. Step 6: Return max_val=9.

Step 1: Initialize max_val = arr[0] = -5. Step 2: i=1: arr[1]=-2 > -5, update max_val=-2. Step 3: i=2: arr[2]=-10 <= -2, no update. Step 4: i=3: arr[3]=-1 > -2, update max_val=-1. Step 5: Return max_val=-1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare and Update Maximum in a Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that compares each element to a running maximum and updates it if a larger element is found.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the heart of the linear scan approach, focusing on the iterative process of maintaining a current maximum. Intuitively, it works by ensuring that after processing each element, the running maximum is the largest among all elements seen so far. The algorithm requires an initial maximum value (provided as input) and an array to iterate through. It loops through each element, comparing it to the current maximum. If the element is greater, the maximum is updated; otherwise, it remains unchanged. This guarantees that by the end, the maximum reflects the largest element in the entire array if the initial maximum is set appropriately (e.g., to the first element). Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of comparison and state update.

connectsTo.reason: This subroutine is directly embedded in the full solution, where the loop handles the bulk of the work

timeComplexity.note: for n elements, as each comparison is constant time, and space is O(1) for storing the maximum

commonMistakes: not updating the maximum correctly (e.g
using < instead of >)
or skipping elements in the loop
explanation.codePattern: def update_max(arr, initial_max): max_val = initial_max; for num in arr: if num > max_val: max_val = num; return max_val

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with max_val = 3. Step 2: Process num=4: 4 > 3, update max_val=4. Step 3: num=1: 1 <= 4, no update. Step 4: num=5: 5 > 4, update max_val=5. Step 5: Return max_val=5.
Step 1: Start with max_val = -3. Step 2: num=-2: -2 > -3, update max_val=-2. Step 3: num=-5: -5 <= -2, no update. Step 4: num=-1: -1 > -2, update max_val=-1. Step 5: Return max_val=-1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining State Through Comparisons
ladders.desc: Understand how to keep track of a value (maximum) and update it based on comparisons with new data.

explanation.prose: This level focuses on the fundamental technique of state maintenance via comparisons, which is central to many algorithms. Intuitively, it works by having a variable that represents the current best candidate (e.g., maximum) and updating it only when a better candidate is found through comparison. This is a form of greedy decision-making: at each step, we decide whether to change our state based on new information. The algorithm involves initializing a state variable, then repeatedly comparing new values to it and updating if the new value is better according to some criterion (here, greater than). This ensures the state always reflects the best seen so far. Time and space are O(1) per comparison, but in context, it scales linearly with the number of comparisons. Connection to L3: It builds on the ability to compare two values.

connectsTo.reason: This logic is applied in the loop where each element is compared to the running maximum

commonMistakes: not initializing the state properly or updating it incorrectly (e.g
always updating without comparison)
explanation.codePattern: state = initial_value; for new_value in data: if new_value > state: state = new_value

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with state = 5. Step 2: Compare new_value=3: 3 <= 5, state remains 5. Step 3: new_value=7: 7 > 5, update state=7. Step 4: new_value=2: 2 <= 7, state remains 7. Step 5: Final state=7.

Step 1: Start with state = -10. Step 2: new_value=-5: -5 > -10, update state=-5. Step 3: new_value=-8: -8 <= -5, state remains -5. Step 4: new_value=-3: -3 > -5, update state=-3. Step 5: Final state=-3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Implement a function that compares two integers and returns the larger one.

explanation.prose: This intermediate concept involves comparing two values to determine which is larger, a basic operation in many algorithms. Intuitively, it works by using relational operators (like >) to evaluate the magnitude of numbers. The algorithm takes two integers as input and returns the greater of the two. If they are equal, either can be returned (typically the first or a designated value). This is a deterministic process based on the properties of integers. Time and space complexity are O(1) since it involves a constant-time comparison and no extra data structures. Connection to L4: It relies on the fundamental operation of using comparison operators.

connectsTo.reason: This comparison is the atomic action used in state maintenance—each update decision hinges on comparing a new value to the current state

commonMistakes: confusing greater than (>) with less than (<)
or not handling equal values consistently
explanation.codePattern: def max_of_two(a, b): if a > b: return a else: return b

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare a=5 and b=3. Step 2: Since 5 > 3, return 5.
Step 1: Compare a=-2 and b=-1. Step 2: Since -1 > -2, return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Using Comparison Operators on Integers
ladders.desc: Practice using greater-than (>) operator to compare two integer values.

explanation.prose: This fundamental operation involves evaluating whether one integer is greater than another, a key skill in programming. Intuitively, it works by leveraging the inherent order of integers on the number line: for any two integers a and b, a > b is true if a is to the right of b on the number line. The algorithm is simply applying the > operator to two inputs, which returns a boolean (true or false). This is a primitive operation in most programming languages, executed in constant time O(1) and using O(1) space. Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of integers and their order.

connectsTo.reason: This operation is used within the comparison function to decide which number is larger

commonMistakes: using the wrong operator (e.g
>= when > is needed) or misunderstanding negative numbers (e.g
thinking -5 > -3)
explanation.codePattern: result = a > b  # returns True if a is greater, else False

examples.trace: Step 1: Evaluate 4 > 2. Step 2: Since 4 is greater than 2, output True.
Step 1: Evaluate -3 > -1. Step 2: Since -3 is less than -1, output False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers and Order
ladders.desc: Learn what integers are and how they can be ordered from smallest to largest.

explanation.prose: This is the simplest prerequisite concept, focusing on the basic understanding of integers and their natural order. Intuitively, integers are whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero) that can be arranged on a number line, where numbers to the right are greater. This order is fundamental because it allows comparisons: for any two integers, one is either greater than, less than, or equal to the other. The algorithm here is conceptual: given two integers, identify which is larger by visualizing their positions on the number line. For example, 3 is greater than 1 because 3 is to the right of 1; -2 is greater than -5 because -2 is to the right of -5. Time and space are not applicable as this is a conceptual exercise.

connectsTo.reason: This understanding enables the use of comparison operators

commonMistakes: confusing the order of negative numbers (e.g., thinking -10 is greater than -
or not recognizing that zero is greater than negative numbers
explanation.codePattern: This is a mental model: on a number line, if a is to the right of b, then a > b

examples.trace: Step 1: Consider the number line: ... 2 ... 5 ... Step 2: Since 5 is to the right of 2, 5 is larger.
Step 1: Consider the number line: ... -7 ... -3 ... Step 2: Since -3 is to the right of -7, -3 is larger.

approaches.name: Divide and Conquer O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively split the array into halves, find the maximum in each half, and combine results. This demonstrates a classic algorithmic paradigm.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) due to recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Maximum Element Using Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that splits array into halves to find maximum element.

explanation.prose: This is the complete implementation of finding the maximum element in an array using the divide and conquer paradigm. The intuition is that by recursively dividing the array into smaller subarrays, we can find the maximum in each half and then combine these results. This approach demonstrates how a problem can be broken down into smaller, more manageable subproblems that are solved independently and then merged.

connectsTo.reason: This is the complete solution that integrates all the building blocks from lower levels

commonMistakes: Incorrect base case handling - forgetting to handle empty arrays or arrays with one element.
Off-by-one errors in midpoint calculation or array indexing
Not properly passing array boundaries in recursive calls
Forgetting to return values from recursive calls
algorithm: 1. Base case: If the array has only one element, return that element as the maximum.
2. Recursive case: Calculate the midpoint of the current array segment.
3. Recursively find the maximum in the left half (from start to midpoint).
4. Recursively find the maximum in the right half (from midpoint+1 to end).
5. Compare the two maximums and return the larger value.

time.note: Each element is visited once during the recursion

space.note: Due to recursion stack depth for balanced splits

examples.trace:
find_max(arr, 0, 4)
  mid = (0+4)//2 = 2
  left_max = find_max(arr, 0, 2)
    mid = (0+2)//2 = 1
    left_max = find_max(arr, 0, 1)
      mid = (0+1)//2 = 0
      left_max = find_max(arr, 0, 0) -> returns 3
      right_max = find_max(arr, 1, 1) -> returns 7
      returns max(3,7) = 7
    right_max = find_max(arr, 2, 2) -> returns 2
    returns max(7,2) = 7
  right_max = find_max(arr, 3, 4)
    mid = (3+4)//2 = 3
    left_max = find_max(arr, 3, 3) -> returns 9
    right_max = find_max(arr, 4, 4) -> returns 1
    returns max(9,1) = 9
  returns max(7,9) = 9

find_max(arr, 0, 4)
  mid = (0+4)//2 = 2
  left_max = find_max(arr, 0, 2)
    mid = (0+2)//2 = 1
    left_max = find_max(arr, 0, 1)
      mid = (0+1)//2 = 0
      left_max = find_max(arr, 0, 0) -> returns -5
      right_max = find_max(arr, 1, 1) -> returns -2
      returns max(-5,-2) = -2
    right_max = find_max(arr, 2, 2) -> returns -8
    returns max(-2,-8) = -2
  right_max = find_max(arr, 3, 4)
    mid = (3+4)//2 = 3
    left_max = find_max(arr, 3, 3) -> returns -1
    right_max = find_max(arr, 4, 4) -> returns -3
    returns max(-1,-3) = -1
  returns max(-2,-1) = -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Maximum Finder for Array Segment
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that finds maximum in a given array segment.

explanation.prose: This is the core recursive function that implements the divide and conquer logic for finding the maximum in a specific segment of an array. The intuition is that we can find the maximum of an array by finding the maximum of its two halves and taking the larger of those two values. This recursive decomposition continues until we reach the base case of a single element.

connectsTo.reason: This function is called by the main solution to process different segments of the array

commonMistakes: Not handling the base case correctly for single-element segments.
Incorrect calculation of midpoint leading to infinite recursion
Off-by-one errors in recursive calls (e.g., using mid instead of mid+1 for right segment)
Not returning values properly from recursive calls
algorithm: 1. Input: array, start index, end index
2. Base case: if start == end, return arr[start]
3. Calculate midpoint = (start + end) // 2
4. Recursively find maximum in left segment (start to midpoint)
5. Recursively find maximum in right segment (midpoint+1 to end)
6. Return max(left_max, right_max)

examples.trace:
find_max_segment(arr, 0, 3)
  mid = (0+3)//2 = 1
  left_max = find_max_segment(arr, 0, 1)
    mid = (0+1)//2 = 0
    left_max = find_max_segment(arr, 0, 0) -> returns 4
    right_max = find_max_segment(arr, 1, 1) -> returns 2
    returns max(4,2) = 4
  right_max = find_max_segment(arr, 2, 3)
    mid = (2+3)//2 = 2
    left_max = find_max_segment(arr, 2, 2) -> returns 7
    right_max = find_max_segment(arr, 3, 3) -> returns 1
    returns max(7,1) = 7
  returns max(4,7) = 7

find_max_segment(arr, 0, 2)
  mid = (0+2)//2 = 1
  left_max = find_max_segment(arr, 0, 1)
    mid = (0+1)//2 = 0
    left_max = find_max_segment(arr, 0, 0) -> returns -3
    right_max = find_max_segment(arr, 1, 1) -> returns -1
    returns max(-3,-1) = -1
  right_max = find_max_segment(arr, 2, 2) -> returns -4
  returns max(-1,-4) = -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divide Array and Compare Maximums
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to split array and compare maximums from halves.
